coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorometric detection of Cu2+ through a hybrid nanoprobe composed of N-doped CQDs and l-cysteine-capped CdSe/ZnS QDs
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhaofa
Zhou, Quanxiu
Sathishkumar, Palanivel
Peng, Liang
Wang, Qunfang
Gu, Feng Long
coredata.dc:description: The present work fabricated a novel dual-emission responsive ratiometric fluorescent hybrid nanoprobe using a mixture of blue fluorescence-emitting N-Doped Carbon Quantum Dots (N-CQDs) and red fluorescence-emitting l-cysteine-capped CdSe/ZnS Quantum Dots (Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs). The addition of Cu2+ resulted in the strong and specific fluorescence quenching in Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs while N-CQDs retained their fluorescence intensity. Further exploration of the nanoprobe showed that the detection limit was 3.78 × 10−9 M and the linear range was below 3.2 × 10−6 M. The nanoprobe possessed excellent anti-interference in the Cu2+ determination and its application was successful in natural water samples. Experimental results suggested that the superficial left cysteine (Lcys) combines with Cu2+ causing static quenching. Results of density functional theory (DFT) computation explained that Cu2+ has the greatest possibility to combine with surficial Lcys and furthermore illustrated the role of the combination in static quenching. The work showed one of origin of the selectivity of the nanoprobe and a potentially guidance to design high selective ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescent hybrid nanoprobe
Quenching mechanism
Copper ions
Density functional theory (DFT)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the lcys-capped CdSe/ZnS QDs (Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs)
Synthesis of the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Detection of Cu2+ by the hybrid ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe
Characterization of N-CQDs and Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs
Fluorescence properties of the N-CQDs and Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs nanoprobe
Selectivity of the N-CQDs/Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs ratiometric fluorescent probe
Analytical application in environment water samples

National Key R&D Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for financial support from the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2017YFB0203403 ). This work was supported by the grant of scientific research fund from Guangzhou city ( 201802010032 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of N-CQDs, (b) TEM images of Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs (inset: diameter of QDs and HRTEM image show clearly the inter-planer spacing), (c) FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs and (d) FTIR spectra of TOPO-CdSe/ZnS, Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs and Lcys.

The fluorescence emission spectra consist of (a) Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs, (b) N-CQDs, (c) N-CQDs/Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs in absence of Cu2+ and (d) N-CQDs/Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs in presence of Cu2+ (N-CQDs: 50 μL, Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs: 50 μL, Cu2+: 3.0 × 10−6 M, Phosphate buffer: 0.1 M, pH = 7.0, λex = 365 nm).

(a) The selectivity of the hybrid nanoprobe in presence of different kinds of metal ions and (b) The selectivity of the hybrid nanoprobe in presence of different kinds of anions (N-CQDs: 50 μL, Lcys-CdSe/ZnSQDs: 50 μL, Cu2+: 3.0 × 10−6 M, the concentrations of other metal ions and anions are 1.0 × 10−5 M, Phosphate buffer: 0.1 M, pH = 7.0).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the ratiometric fluorescent hybrid nanoprobe in presence of Cu2+ at various concentrations under optimal conditions and (b) The linear relationship of (F612/F450)0/(F612/F450) versus the concentration of Cu2+(N-CQDs: 50 μL, Lcys-CdSe/ZnSQDs: 50 μL, Phosphate buffer: 0.1 M, pH = 7.0).
(a) Absorption spectra of N-CQDs/Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs under different concentrations of Cu2+ and (b) TEM of N-CQDs/Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs in presence of Cu2+.
Photoluminescence mechanism and quenching mechanism of Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs.
The optimized structure of Cu aquo complex and Cu-Mcys complex, in which white refers to H, grey to C, blue to N, red to O, yellow to S and brown to Cu.
Comparison of the nanoprobes in this work with various state-of-the-art fluorescent sensors.
Recoveries in spiked lake, river and tap water samples determined by the hybrid nanoprobe.
Interaction free energies (ΔG) and LUMO energies of ion-Mcys complex.

row.entry: ion-Mcys complex with no deprotonated Mcys
ion-Mcys complex with one deprotonated Mcys
ion-Mcys complex with two deprotonated Mcys

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorometric detection of Cu2+ through a hybrid nanoprobe composed of N-doped CQDs and l-cysteine-capped CdSe/ZnS QDs

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work fabricated a novel dual-emission responsive ratiometric fluorescent hybrid nanoprobe using a mixture of blue fluorescence-emitting N-Doped Carbon Quantum Dots (N-CQDs) and red fluorescence-emitting l-cysteine-capped CdSe/ZnS Quantum Dots (Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs). The addition of Cu2+ resulted in the strong and specific fluorescence quenching in Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs while N-CQDs retained their fluorescence intensity. Further exploration of the nanoprobe showed that the detection limit was 3.78 × 10−9 M and the linear range was below 3.2 × 10−6 M. The nanoprobe possessed excellent anti-interference in the Cu2+ determination and its application was successful in natural water samples. Experimental results suggested that the superficial left cysteine (Lcys) combines with Cu2+ causing static quenching. Results of density functional theory (DFT) computation explained that Cu2+ has the greatest possibility to combine with surficial Lcys and furthermore illustrated the role of the combination in static quenching. The work showed one of origin of the selectivity of the nanoprobe and a potentially guidance to design high selective ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper (Cu) is a transition metal, which is considered vital to human tissues, bone formation, and cellular respiration [1]. However, the excessive concentrations of Cu2+ can lead liver damage, Alzheimer's disease, and Parkinson's disease [2,3]. According to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the concentration of Cu2+ in the primary water should not exceed 2.0 × 10−5 M. Therefore, it is important to maintain the water Cu2+ under the EPA limit for human consumption. So far, conventional analytical techniques, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [4], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [5], and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6] have been employed for the detection of Cu2+ in the water. Although, these methods exhibit high sensibility and selectivity, their applicability is limited due to excessive time consumption and cost. Among the methods, the fluorescence mediated metal detection techniques have proved to be an ideal method for Cu2+ detection because of their high sensitivity, rapidity, and simplicity [7,8], in which the fluorescence responsive quantum dots (QDs) are semiconductor nanomaterials with outstanding optical properties. QDs have gotten a lot of attention in fluorescence analysis because of their high quantum yield, light stability, egregious surface chemical properties and ligand binding ability [9–12].

Chen et al. used QDs as fluorescent probes for the first time to detect Cu2+ [13], followed by a serial studies that focused on the detection of Cu2+ by quenching or enhancing the fluorescence intensity of single-emission QDs. Reiss et al. [9] mentioned that core/shell systems such as CdSe/ZnS not only retain their core optically active but also reduce their sensitivity to environmental changes. Furthermore, Oliveros et al. [14] referred that the shell ZnS prevents the leak of heavy elements mental cations from the core CdSe. Xie et al. employed CdSe/ZnS QDs as selective Cu2+ probe [15]. However, due to the lack of built-in correction, single-emission spectra of fluorescent probes are usually affected by the probe concentration, excitation intensity, and efficiency of the emission collection. The ratiometric fluorescent hybrid nanoprobe based on dual-emission or multi-emission has an effective internal reference system, which can effectively overcome the above shortcomings. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescent hybrid nanoprobes provide more sensitive and selective detection as well as visual analysis for metal ions [16–18]. Yao et al. synthesized two sizes of CdTe QDs as a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe to determine Cu2+ [11]. Carbon dots and their dopants have advantages of chemical inertness, lack of optical blinking, low photo-bleaching, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, and so they can be chosen as reference signal in ratiometric fluorescent probe [19–21]. For example, Cao et al. prepared a hybrid ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe composed of blue-emitting carbon dots and red-emitting carboxymethyldithiocarbamate modified CdSe/ZnS QDs to detect Hg2+ [22]. Wu et al. utilized a mixture of blue-emitting silicon-nitrogen-doped carbon QDs and red-emitting N-acetyl-l-cysteine-capped CdTe QDs as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of Ag+ [23]. Most of these studies have used small molecules containing sulfhydryl groups as ligands to enhance water solubility [24]. Wang et al. prepared thioacetamide capped CdS QDs for rapid detection of Cu2+ [25]. From the previous work, we notice that Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs has excellent stability and solubility in water while N-CQDs can provide stable and strong reference signal in aqueous solution. Thus, we proposed that addition of N-CQDs potentially does not disturb the fluorescence of Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs, which means the feasibility of the hybrid nanoprobe Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs and N-CQDs. Furthermore, the addition may improve the anti-inference, selectivity and provides visual analysis of detection of Cu2+.

The surface ligands possibly play an important role in the selectivity of nanoprobes. Wu et al. and Zhao et al. proposed that combination of superficial ligands of QDs and quenchers results in static quenching of QDs to specific metal cation [17,26]. However, few reports have concerned about the reason that a superficial ligand of QDs tends to interact with a specific metal cation but not other cations. So, we used DFT computation to study the binding potencies between Lcys and Cu2+ as well as other ions and their relation to the selectivity of the nanoprobe.

In the present work, Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs and N-Doped CQDs were mixed to form the hybrid nanoprobe. A series experiments were conducted to investigate its fluorescent performance, anti-interference, sensitivity and selectivity for detection of Cu2+ and its applicability was also researched. Experimental results showed feasibility of the hybrid nanoprobe and suggested that superficial L-cys binds to Cu2+. To understand that Lcys combines to Cu2+ but not other ions, we applied DFT calculation to research interaction free energy between ligands and Cu2+ as well as other interference ions. And furthermore, for understanding of relationship between combination and the selectivity, the mechanism of the fluorescence quenching was studied. The above work might give guidance on design of high selective ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe with visual analysis.

Trisodium citrate, acetone, potassium hydroxide, sodium hydroxide, ammonium chloride, hexane and anhydrous methanol were bought from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Company (A.R, Tianjin, China). Stearic acid (SA) was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd (98%, Beijing, China). Chloroform was purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (A.R, Guangzhou, China). Paraffin liquid, cadmium oxide (CdO, 99.99%), selenium powder (Se, 99.99%), zinc oxide (ZnO, 99.99%), trioctylphosphine (TOP, 90%), trioctylphosphine oxide (TOPO, 98%), octadecylamine (ODA, 90%), oleic acid (OA, A.R), 1-octadecene (ODE, G.C), sulfur powder (S, 99.95%), l-cysteine (Lcys, 99%), Tris (hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane (ACS) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin (Shanghai, China). PBS buffer was purchased from Shenggong Bioengineering Company (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was provided by Millipore water purification system purchased from Guangzhou Huanghe Company (Guangzhou, China).

The optical properties of QDs were studied by using a Shimadzu UV-2700 Spectrophotometer. The fluorescence measurements were carried out using a Hitachi F-4600 spectrofluorometric with a xenon lamp and a quartz cuvette (path length of 1 cm) at room temperature. Both the excitation and emission band slits were 5 nm and the excitation wavelength were 365 nm. The particle size and morphology of QDs were measured by the FEI Talos F200X transmission electron microscope with an information limit of 0.12 nm. The elemental compositions of QDs were determined by X-ray Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectra (FTIR) in KBr were recorded using a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer. The zeta potentials of QDs were measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 particle analyzer. pH was measured using a PHS-ST10 written test pH meter (Ohaus). Fluorescence lifetime was acquired using an Edinburgh FLS980 Spectrofluorometer. The QDs were freeze-dried using the Shanghai Bilang Freeze Dryer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the lcys-capped CdSe/ZnS QDs (Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs)
ce:section.ce:para: CdSe/ZnS QDs were prepared according to methods described in our previous work [27]. The Lcys-KOH methanol solution was prepared by dissolving 0.2 g of Lcys and 0.3 g of potassium hydroxide in 4 mL of methanol by ultra-sonication. This resulting solution was added to CdSe/ZnS QDs in chloroform, and then 4 mL of ultra-pure water was added. Finally, this solution was stirred at room temperature for 1 h. The Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs were purified by acetone and chloroform by centrifuging for 10 min at 7000 rpm. The final yield of the QDs was calculated as 8.08 g L−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Trisodium citrate (0.1 g), and NH4Cl (0.5 g) dissolved in 5 mL of ultra-pure water were autoclaved at 180 °C for 4 h, and then cooled to room temperature. The mixture was purified by dialysis using the dialysis membrane (MWCO 5000 Da, Yibo) for 24 h [28]. Finally, N-CQDs were freeze dried. The N-CQDs powder was dispersed into ultra-pure water with a concentration of 0.1 g L−1 for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ by the hybrid ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The hybrid N-CQDs/Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs nanoprobe was prepared by mixing 50 μL of N-CQDs, 50 μL of Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs in 500 μL of PBS buffer (0.1 M; pH = 7.0). The mixed solution was diluted to 1.0 mL with ultra-pure water, and different concentrations of Cu2+ were added to the solution that was equilibrated for 1 min. Both of the excitation and emission band slits were fixed to 5 nm at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, and the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe was measured at the emission wavelengths of 450 nm and 612 nm.

Tap water, river water, and lake water were collected from the Pearl River near the main entrance of South China Normal University (SCNU) in Guangzhou Higher Education Mega Center (HEMC) and the Inkstone Lake of SCUN in HEMC, respectively, and transported to the laboratory. The river water and lake water sample were collected by 500 mL mineral water bottle. The water samples were centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 20 min, and then filtered through 0.45 μm micro strainers (Bukman Biology) to remove the impurities. The Cu2+ in water samples was determined by the standard addition method [29] with a concentration of 3.0 × 10−6 M.

Gaussian16 software package [30] was used to perform the all theoretical calculations. Equilibrium geometries of the ion-aquo complex and ion-complex were fully optimized, followed by harmonic analysis calculation for confirmation of their minima. All geometries were optimized via functional PBE0-D3 [31,32]. The basis set def2-TZVP [33] was used for elements K, Ca, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Br, Mo, Ag, Cd, I, Hg, and Pb, while the def2-SVP [33] basis set was employed for H, C, N, O, F, Na, Mg, Al, P, S and Cl. Because for elements in 4th period and the above, def2-TZVP will give a more accuracy result of dative bond length [34]. The interaction in aqueous solution was described by a polarized continuum model (PCM) [35]. On account of carbonyls and amino groups as weak ligands, the high spin state of transition metal cations was chosen.

Interaction free energies between ions and cysteine were calculated according to the following formula:

means free energy of ion-Mcys complex, 6 waters cluster, 1 hydroxide and 5 waters cluster, 2 hydroxides and 4 waters cluster, ion-aquo complex, Mcys and H+-aquo complex. ΔG1 is the interaction free energy under the case that ion-Mcys complex has no deprotonated Mcys, ΔG2 is under the case that ion-Mcys complex has one deprotonated Mcys, ΔG3 is under the case that ion-Mcys complex has two deprotonated Mcys, ΔG4 is under the case that Fe3+ and Cr3+ interact with Mcys and ΔG5 is under the case that Al3+ interacts with Mcys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-CQDs and Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 a, the N-CQDs are spherical with excellent dispersibility and the inset shows the average particle size was 3.11 nm (inset in Fig. 1 (a)). Fig. 1 (b) shows the uniform spherical particles with good dispersion and crystal lattice structures for Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs. The obtained interplanar spacing from HRTEM analysis was found to be 0.41 nm for QDs. The particle size distribution of the illustration shows that the average size was 4.15 nm. In Fig. S1, EDS spectra of QDs shows that the N-CQDs contained the elements such as C, N, and O, and the Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs contained the elements including C, O, Cd, Se, Zn, and S.

The FTIR measurements were performed to investigate the chemical structure of ligands on the surface of QDs. Fig. 1 (c) displays the FTIR spectrum of the N-CQDs. The peaks at 3118 cm−1 and 3028 cm−1 stand for amino-group and hydroxyl, 1766 cm−1 for carbonyl, 1577 cm−1 for imino-group, and 1395 cm−1 for C–N. The FTIR spectra of the TOPO-CdSe/ZnS QDs are showed in Fig. 1 (d). The peaks at 2915 cm−1 and 2848 cm−1 contribute to the asymmetric and symmetrical stretching of methylene, and the peaks at 1471 cm−1 to the stretching deformation of the methylene group near P, and PO. The peaks at 3310 cm−1 and 1635 cm−1 illustrate that the sample contained water. Comparison of the FTIR of the Lcys, TOPO-CdSe/ZnS QDs and the Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs (Fig. 1 (d)) illustrates that the characteristic peaks of the ligand TOPO of the TOPO-CdSe/ZnS QDs disappear while some peaks of Lcys emerge in Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs. This exhibits a successful substitution of TOPO by Lcys. The peak at 2551 cm−1 in Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs vanishes, which suggests that Zn–S bonds were formed between the thio-group of Lcys and Zn2+ on the surface of CdSe/ZnS QDs. The peak at 3253 cm−1 is contributed to the carboxyl and amino-group of the ligand Lcys on the surface. Furthermore, the peak is broadened, which indicates that water molecules binded on the surface of Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs. And thus, the FTIR results affirm that the Lcys capped on the surface of the CdSe/ZnS QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties of the N-CQDs and Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the fluorescence emission spectra of Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs in (a), N-CQDs in (b), N-CQDs/Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs in absence of Cu2+ in (c) and N-CQDs/Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs in presence of Cu2+ in (d). The peak of N-CQDs is situated at 446 nm and the peak of Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs at 612 nm. Two peaks of the ratiometric nanoprobe are located at 450 nm and 612 nm respectively. A 4 nm red-shift of the fluorescence emission peak of N-CQDs appears which suggests that amino-group and carboxyl groups on the surface of QDs were coupled through electrostatic interaction. The particle size of N-CQDs was smaller than Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs, so a slight shift happened in the N-CQDs emission spectrum. Although the fluorescence intensity of the mixture of N-CQDs/Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs slightly decreases because of the interaction between N-CQDs and Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs, it has enough sensitivity as a ratiometric fluorescent hybrid nanoprobe. And meanwhile, the zeta potential of N-CQDs and Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs are −28.6 mV and −32 mV respectively (in Fig. S2), while zeta potential of the hybrid QDs is −33.1 mV ( Fig. S6). The reduction of zeta potential shows the existence of the repulsion between N-CQDs and Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs, which confirms the stability of the hybrid QDs. After an addition of Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity of peaks at 450 nm remains unchanged, while the peaks at 612 nm are almost quenched. At the same time, the color of the solution changes from pink to blue under a 365 nm UV lamp (the inset of Fig. 2). Motivated by the above observations of the fluorescence response, a ratiometric fluorescent hybrid nanoprobe was developed to determine Cu2+, in which Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs were used as the response signal and N-CQDs as the reference signal.

In order to achieve the ratiometric detection and reduce the interference from different experimental conditions, the volume ratio of N-CQDs and Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs, pH, reaction time, and selectivity were optimized.

The influence of the volume ratio of N-CQDs and Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs (VN-CQDs/VQDs) on detection is shown in Figure S3. As the volume ratio of VN-CQDs/VQDs increases, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F612/F450), meaning the ratio of fluorescence at 612 nm and at 450 nm, shows a noticeable decrease until VN-CQDs/VQDs is up to 5:5. Then, as the VN-CQDs/VQDs increase, the change in F612/F450 decreases (Figure S3 (a)). Figure S3 (b) shows the effect of volume ratio on the quenching efficiency under different Cu2+ concentrations. In low Cu2+ concentrations, the volume ratio VN-CQDs/VQDs has almost no effect on the quenching efficiency (black column) represented by relative fluorescence intensity ratio (F612/F450)0/(F612/F450). An obvious fluctuation of (F612/F450)0/(F612/F450) arises when the Cu2+ concentration is 3.0 × 10−6 M. The quenching efficiency of (F612/F450)0/(F612/F450) (red column) reaches the maximum value at volume ratio 5:5. Thus, the volume ratio of N-CQDs/Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs of 5:5 was selected for the hybrid nanoprobe.

The pH of the solution may influence the stability of quencher and size of Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs. And thus, for the investigation of the influence of the pH and types of buffer solutions, their influence on the fluorescence and the quenching efficiency of the hybrid nanoprobe were studied. In Figure S4 (a), the fluorescence intensity ratio (F612/F450) of the hybrid nanoprobe in PBS buffer and HEPES buffer is shown in the range of pH 5.0–10.0. In both buffers with an increase in pH, the F612/F450 increases to the maximum point at pH 7.5 and slightly fluctuates near the maximal point above pH 7.5, which implies that high acidity strongly influences the fluorescence while the type of buffer solution has almost no impact on it. The reason is that in acidic solution, the protonation of the thiol on the surface of the Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs releases Lcys from the surface of the QDs. And then the detachment of Lcys weakens electrostatic and steric repulsion between the QDs, which leads to the aggregation of the QDs. Finally, with increscent size of the QDs, the fluorescence vanishes and even further the precipitation occurs [36]. After the addition of Cu2+, the quenching efficiency of the nanoprobe is related to the type of buffer solution. Figure S4 (b) shows that the quenching efficiency (F612/F450)0/(F612/F450) is higher in PBS buffer solution than in HEPES buffer solution. And thus, PBS buffer solution with a pH of 7.0 was selected for the hybrid nanoprobe.

In consideration of sufficient contact between fluorophore and quencher, regardless of Cu2+ concentration, the hybrid nanoprobe's quenching efficiencies (F612/F450)0/(F612/F450) reach their maximum after 1 min (Fig. S5). And thus, the equilibrium time of the hybrid nanoprobe was chosen as 1 min.

Li et al. [37] suggested that in room temperature the fluorescence of CdSe/ZnS QDs is not quenched but in high temperature (> 470 K) the fluorescence is completely quenched. Since our experiments were in room temperature, the influence of the temperature was neglected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the N-CQDs/Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-interference of the hybrid nanoprobe in the presence of common ions in water was examined. Under the optimum conditions, the quenching efficiencies (F612/F450)0/(F612/F450) of the hybrid nanoprobe were investigated in the presence of metal ions (Ba2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Ca2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Fe3+) and anions (Br−, F−, I−, MnO4 −, PO43−, SO42−, MoO4 2−, CO3 2−) respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 , the quenching efficiency is high for Cu2+ but is low for the other ions (black column). However, after the addition of Cu2+, all the quenching efficiencies are roughly the same (red column). And thus, Cu2+ quenched the fluorescence of the Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs most efficiently among the tested ions, and the other ions did not disturb the quenching of the fluorescence by Cu2+. This showed that hybrid nanoprobe is very specific for detecting of Cu2+ and has a high level of selectivity.

The performance of the ratiometric fluorescent hybrid nanoprobe in a diluted concentration of Cu2+ was in research. In Fig. 4 (a), it is observed that the fluorescence intensity of the Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs gradually decreases with an increase of Cu2+ concentration while the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs is almost invariant in different Cu2+ concentrations, which indicates that the fluorescence ratio F612/F450 is related to Cu2+ concentration. In Fig. 4 (b), it is showed that the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F612/F450)0/(F612/F450) of the ratiometric fluorescent hybrid nanoprobe accords with Stern-Volmer equation in diluted Cu2+ concentration. The Stern-Volmer relationship is as follows:
(4)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of the QDs in the presence and absence of metal ions respectively, [Q] is the concentration of metal ions, and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. It is displayed that the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (F612/F450) has an egregious linear relationship with the Cu2+ concentration below 3.2 × 10−6 M. Its linear relationship is y = 0.21808x + 0.88757 with linear correlation coefficient 0.99793, the standard errors of slope 0.00275, intercept 0.06852 and the detection limit 3.78 × 10−9 M (3σ/k, σ is the standard deviation and k is the slope of the fitted curve) [38]. In addition, the upward departure is contribution of dynamic quenching [17]. The linear range of this method is wide enough and the detection limit is lower than the standard of Cu2+ in EPA drinking water (2.0 × 10−5 M). Therefore, it is feasible to use this hybrid nanoprobe to detect Cu2+.

A comparative research of some previously reported results is presented in Table 1 . It suggests that our nanoprobe has higher sensitivity to Cu2+ than other state-of-the-art fluorescent sensors and although its comparatively narrow range, it is still suitable for the detection Cu2+in natural water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application in environment water samples
ce:section.ce:para: For further exploration of the feasibility of the hybrid nanoprobe in detection of Cu2+, the standard addition method was used to analyze the content of Cu2+ in lake, river and tap water samples. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery rate of standard addition is between 90.00 and 106.50%, and the relative standard deviation is between 0.41 and 3.21%, which shows that the method has high precision and meets the requirements of trace analysis. Therefore, it is a reliable and practical to employ this hybrid nanoprobe to detect Cu2+ in environment water samples.

Since fluorescence quenching of the hybrid nanoprobe was irrelevant to N-CQDs but relevant to Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs, the quenching mechanism of the Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs by Cu2+ was studied. The photoluminescence of QDs is mainly divided into exciton luminescence and surface defect luminescence. The fluorescence of QDs is closely related to nature of QDs, size of QDs, surface defects and the properties and density of surface ligands [43]. The main causes of fluorescence quenching is non-radiative recombination favored by ions, electron transfer, cation exchange and ligand competition [44]. According to previous reports, if the fluorescence of QDs is quenched only by an electron transfer, the fluorescence peak and absorption peak do not shift [45,46]. But, if the fluorescence of QDs is quenched due to the adsorption of chemical substances on the surface of QDs, the fluorescence peak and absorption peak is significantly shifted [47–49].

In our study, the peak locations of the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the N-CQDs/Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs remain almost invariant in the presence of Cu2+ shown in Figs. 4 (a) and 5 (a). The invariant peak locations of the spectra show that Cu2+ was not directly adsorbed on the surface of the CdSe QDs, because it does not cause the redistribution of electron density of the QDs. As depicted in Fig. S7, the color and state of the QDs solution do not change after the addition of Cu2+, which indicates no formation of other new substances. The zeta potential of the nanoprobe reduces from about −33.1 mV to −30.8 mV after addition of Cu2+ (Fig. S6), which eliminates the possibility that Cu2+ directly adsorbed on the surface of the CdSe QDs and the QDs aggregated. In the TEM of Fig. 5 (b), the Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs are monodispersed without aggregation, which excludes the possibility that fluorescence is quenched by aggregation. And FTIR demonstrates that Lcys was adsorbed on the surface of the QDs through its thio-group. On one hand, there was an electrostatic force between the negatively charged Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs and Cu2+, and on the other hand, Lcys contained carboxyl and amino groups with strong affinity to Cu2+. And thus Cu2+ may coordinate to Lcys. Therefore, it was concluded that the static quenching is caused by the coordination of Cu2+ with Lcys on the surface of Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs, which results in non-radiative energy dissipation displayed in Fig. 6 . In order to further understand that Lcys binds to Cu2+ but not the other ions, a theoretical computation was carried out.

According to the above experiment, the Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs only interacted with Cu2+ through superficial Lcys. And meanwhile, the orbitals of CdSe/ZnS QDs and ion-Lcys complexes are quite independent [50,51]. So, the ion-Lcys complex was segregated from the complex ion-Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QD. Since Lcys tightens to CdSe/ZnS QDs through thio-group, its thio-group was methylated, modifying Lcys as Mcys.

For the most stable structures of ion-Mcys complexes, the complexes with 3 ligands including 0, 1, 2 and 3 deprotonated Mcys were calculated respectively and the most stable complex was chosen from complex with 0, 1, 2 and 3 deprotonated Mcys. Demonstrated in Fig. 7 and Fig. S9, transition metal complexes present in octahedron while other metal complexes are of various structures such as triangle, tetrahedron and square pyramid. For most anion complexes, the structures are irregular. Interestingly, it is observed that metal cations in the 4th periodic combine with carboxyl and animo-group equivalently while those in 5th and 6th tend to tight with animo-group rather than carboxyl, which has been confirmed by F. M. Al-Sogair et al. [52].

For ion-Mcys complexes listed in Table 3 , interaction free energy of Cu(Mcys)3 is lower than those of other ion-Mcys complexes. And thus among Cu2+ and the other interfering ions, Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs extremely tended to react with Cu2+. So, only the Cu2+-Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs complex was formed but did not other ion-Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs complexes, which is necessary condition of the fluorescence quenching only in presence of Cu2+.

For further understanding the relationship between the combination of the Lcys as well as Cu2+ and the selectivity of the nanoprobe, lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) of ion-Mcys complexes were also computed. As shown in Table 3, except of Al (Mcys)3 3+, Cr (Mcys)3 2+, Fe(III) (Mcys)3 2+ and MnO4(Mcys)3, the LUMO of Cu(Mcys)3 is the lowest among LUMOs of other ion-Mcys complex. Since superficial Lcys only reacts to Cu2+ complex, excited electrons of CdSe/ZnS QDs only transfer to the LUMO of Cu2+-Lcys in complex Cu2+-Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs, resulting in the fluorescence quenching [53]. In addition, the interaction of other ions with Lcys is so weak that the transfer number of the excited electron is too low to allow occurrence of the fluorescence quenching but just leading to the slightly decrease of the intensity. So, the theoretical calculation relates the combination to the selectivity that the fluorescence of the hybrid nanoprobe was quenched only in presence of Cu2+.

In this work, Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs and N-CQDs were synthesized and mixed to form the hybrid nanoprobe. In the hybrid nanoprobe, Cu2+ quenched the fluorescence of Lcys-CdSe/ZnS QDs while N-CQDs were inert to Cu2+ and their fluorescence did not interfere each other. The hybrid nanoprobe showed high selectivity and sensitive to Cu2+ in aqueous solution. Its application in the detection of Cu2+ in natural water samples was satisfactory. The experiment thus showed the feasibility of the hybrid nanoprobe and exhibited that superficial Lcys of the nanoprobe interacts with Cu2+ causing static quenching. Furthermore, DFT computation explained the reason that Lcys only combines with Cu2+ but no other ions. Because Cu2+ only reacts with superficial Lcys of CdSe/ZnS QDs and excited electron of CdSe/ZnS QDs only transfers to accessibly low LUMO of Cu2+-Lcys, DFT calculation showed that static quenching of the fluorescence of the nanoprobe only occurs in presence of Cu2+. So, the computational results revealed one of origin of the selectivity that nanoprobe only quenched by some specific ions. The work gave a potential guidance to design high selective ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes with visual analysis.

All the authors of this work have approved to this submission. This manuscript has not been published and is not under consideration for publication elsewhere. No conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript and it is approved by all authors for publication. We really appreciate your consideration of our manuscript for the publication to your journal.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support from the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2017YFB0203403). This work was supported by the grant of scientific research fund from Guangzhou city (201802010032).
